planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in very well-drained, lean, sandy to gritty, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; bilberry cactus is native to dry, rocky hillsides of central Mexico and requires essentially no supplemental irrigation once established in appropriate soil — overwatering is the primary cause of failure.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, sandy to gritty soil in full sun; water sparingly — once every 2–3 weeks in summer, almost none in winter. Myrtillocactus geometrizans (bilberry cactus or garambulla) is a striking Mexican columnar cactus forming a large, candelabra-like tree cactus with multiple blue-green columnar branches — the blue-grey waxy coating on the branches gives it an extraordinary, almost artificial blue-steel color that makes it one of the most ornamentally distinctive columnar cacti for full-sun landscaping in warm, dry climates. The columns have 6 prominent ribs with clusters of short spines. The plant produces abundant small, grape-sized, dark-purple to blue-black fruits called garambullos in Mexico — they are sweet, mildly flavored, and eaten fresh or made into aguas frescas; they are among the most economically important wild cactus fruits in Mexico. The flowers are small, white, and produced in spring. Established plants are quite drought-tolerant and cold-hardy to about 25°F. An outstanding specimen or hedge cactus for dry, alkaline, sunny gardens in zone 9–12.
sources: Purdue University — New Crop Resources — Cactus Fruits

planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in spring in very well-drained, lean, rocky, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; fishhook barrel cactus is native to the rocky desert slopes of the Sonoran Desert in Arizona and Mexico and is one of the most cold-hardy large barrel cacti — tolerating temperatures to 15°F when established in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, rocky, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; established plants require no supplemental irrigation in most dry climates. Ferocactus wislizeni (fishhook barrel cactus) is one of the most architecturally bold native cacti of the Sonoran Desert — a large, cylindrical cactus reaching 4–6 feet tall and 18–30 inches in diameter, bearing dramatic, heavy, hooked central spines up to 3 inches long (the 'fishhook' that gives it its name) in red, yellow, or reddish-brown surrounded by flat, cross-banded lateral spines in a striking radial pattern around each areole. In late summer–fall, the plant produces rings of orange to yellow flowers at the crown — a reliable late-season bloomer when the desert has little else flowering. The bright-yellow, waxy, pineapple-shaped fruits persist long after the flowers and are eaten by many desert animals. A characteristic name: the central spine curves like a fishhook and was used by Indigenous peoples for that purpose. Famous for 'leaning south' — mature barrel cacti in Arizona often lean toward the southwest, apparently tracking the sun. An outstanding specimen plant for hot, dry, alkaline gardens.
sources: University of Arizona Extension — Native Plants

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after last frost in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; ashwagandha grows rapidly in hot, dry conditions and can be grown as a long-lived perennial in zone 9 or as an annual in cooler climates — roots are harvested after 150–180 days when dried-out looking berries are present.

directions: Direct sow in warm soil in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; thin to 24 inches; harvest roots when berries form on the plant and begin to dry. Withania somnifera (ashwagandha or Indian ginseng) is one of the most important medicinal plants in Ayurvedic medicine — a short-lived perennial shrub native to the dry regions of India, North Africa, and the Mediterranean, producing an extensive root system with thick, fleshy, woody roots that are the primary medicinal product. The roots contain withanolides, a class of steroidal lactones that are the subject of extensive clinical research showing adaptogenic, stress-reducing, anti-inflammatory, and potential cognitive-protective effects. Ashwagandha root powder and extracts are among the most widely sold herbal supplements globally, used for stress management, anxiety reduction, athletic performance enhancement, and cognitive support. The plant itself is an attractive, somewhat felty-leaved, branching shrub with small, inconspicuous flowers and small, tomato-like red berries enclosed in paper lantern-like calyces. Harvest roots 5–6 months after transplanting. The plant is in the nightshade family (Solanaceae) — the berries are mildly toxic.
sources: University of Maryland Medical Center — Ashwagandha
Indian Council of Agricultural Research — Withania somnifera

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost or transplant in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; kalmegh thrives in the heat and humidity of the Gulf Coast summer and is one of the most important medicinal herbs in Indian Ayurvedic and traditional Chinese medicine, used for immune support and respiratory infections.

directions: Direct sow or transplant in moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; harvest aerial parts when plants begin to flower for maximum andrographolide content. Andrographis paniculata (kalmegh, green chiretta, or king of bitters) is one of the most important and widely researched medicinal plants in Asian traditional medicine systems — a tropical Asian annual herb producing intensely bitter leaves (among the bitterest of any plant) containing the bioactive diterpene compound andrographolide. Clinical research has documented significant efficacy for andrographolide in reducing the severity and duration of upper respiratory infections (common cold and flu) — it is one of the better-supported herbal remedies for viral respiratory illness. The plant is used extensively in Ayurvedic medicine (as kalmegh), traditional Chinese medicine, and Southeast Asian folk medicine for fever, liver conditions, and infections. Despite its extreme bitterness, the plant is consumed as an herbal tea, tincture, and tablet supplement throughout South and Southeast Asia. The plant grows as an upright, branching annual with lance-shaped leaves and small, white, pink-speckled flowers; it is well-adapted to hot, humid conditions and grows vigorously through Gulf Coast summers.
sources: University of Maryland Medical Center — Andrographis
Indian Council of Agricultural Research — Andrographis

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full shade; elephant foot is a native woodland understory perennial of the Gulf Coastal Plain and eastern US that thrives in the dappled shade of pine and hardwood forests — an important nectar plant for native butterflies that is rarely available in nurseries but easy to grow from seed in shaded spots.

directions: Direct sow in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part to full shade; thin to 12 inches and allow to self-seed. Elephantopus tomentosus (elephant foot or tobacco weed) is a native perennial wildflower of eastern North American woodlands and the Gulf Coastal Plain — common in the pine and hardwood forest understory from Virginia through Florida and Texas but rarely cultivated, despite being a genuinely useful and attractive shade-garden native. The plant produces a basal rosette of large (6–10 inch), broadly oval, hairy, slightly crinkled leaves (the 'elephant foot' shape that gives it its name) and branching flower stalks 18–30 inches tall in late summer–fall bearing small, lavender-purple, composite flower heads loosely clustered at the branch tips. The flowers, while individually small, are produced in loose, graceful clusters and are strongly attractive to native bees and skippers. A long-lived, easily self-seeding perennial for woodland and shade-garden naturalization, elephant foot is an excellent native companion for ferns, coral bells, and other shade plants in Gulf Coast understory gardens. Essentially maintenance-free in suitable conditions.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Native Wildflowers
North Carolina State Extension — Native Shade Plants
